0:99285.title: No Surprises Act (Effective January 1, 2022)
0:99285.citation: Emergency services: Patients cannot be balance-billed for emergency care at any facility, in-network or out-of-network.
0:99285.why_it_applies: This is an emergency department visit at Princeton General Hospital, and the No Surprises Act explicitly protects patients from being charged beyond their in-network cost-sharing amount for emergency services regardless of facility or provider network status.

0:99285.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
0:99285.citation: A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive. Use CareGap's CMS PFS pricing tool to compare each CPT code's charge against the Medicare-allowed amount.
0:99285.why_it_applies: At 16.7x the Medicare-allowed rate, this charge far exceeds the 3-5x threshold flagged as potentially excessive in the knowledge base, giving the patient clear grounds to formally dispute this line item through the provider's billing department and, if unresolved, through their insurer or the State Attorney General.

1:71046.why_it_applies: This was an emergency department visit, so the No Surprises Act directly limits what the patient can be charged to their in-network cost-sharing amount, regardless of provider network status. A charge of $425 for a service with a Medicare rate of $33.28 warrants scrutiny under this protection.

1:71046.title: Common Billing Errors to Check
1:71046.citation: A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.

1:71046.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
1:71046.citation: I've reviewed my itemized bill and CPT code [XXXXX] appears to be [upcoded/duplicated/higher than the fair market rate]. Can you review this charge?
1:71046.why_it_applies: The 12.8x markup on this chest X-ray provides clear grounds to formally dispute the charge by contacting the billing department, referencing CPT 71046 and the Medicare benchmark rate, and escalating to the insurer or State Insurance Commissioner if unresolved.

letter.subject: Formal Dispute of Excessive Charges — Account #931058 — Michael K. Patel — Date of Service 02/28/2024
letter.body:
Date: [Today's Date]

From:
Michael K. Patel
[Your Address]

Re: Formal Medical Bill Dispute
Patient Name: Michael K. Patel
Date of Service: February 28, 2024
Account Number: 931058
Insurance: BlueCross PPO
Total Amount Billed: $5,110.00

To the Princeton General Hospital Billing Department,

I am writing to formally dispute several charges appearing on the bill associated with the above-referenced account. After conducting a careful review of the itemized charges and comparing them against published Medicare-allowed amounts using the CMS Physician Fee Schedule, I have identified three line items that appear to reflect significant and unjustified overcharges. I am requesting a thorough review, correction, and written response within 30 days of receipt of this letter.

DISPUTED CHARGES

1. CPT 99285 — Emergency Department Visit, Level 5 (High Severity)
Amount Billed: $2,850.00
Medicare-Allowed Amount: $170.73
Multiplier: 16.7x the Medicare rate
This charge is approximately 16.7 times the Medicare-allowed amount for the same service. A charge at this magnitude is not consistent with reasonable or customary billing practices and warrants immediate review for potential upcoding or billing error.

2. CPT 71046 — Chest X-Ray, 2 Views
Amount Billed: $425.00
Medicare-Allowed Amount: $33.28
Multiplier: 12.8x the Medicare rate
This charge is approximately 12.8 times the Medicare-allowed amount. Industry guidance consistently identifies charges exceeding 3 to 5 times the Medicare rate as potentially excessive. This charge far exceeds that threshold.

3. CPT 93010 — Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)
Amount Billed: $310.00
Medicare-Allowed Amount: $66.56
Multiplier: 4.7x the Medicare rate
This charge is approximately 4.7 times the Medicare-allowed amount and falls at the upper boundary of what is generally considered reasonable. I am requesting documentation that substantiates this charge at the billed rate.

APPLICABLE LEGAL PROTECTIONS

This visit constitutes emergency medical care. Under the No Surprises Act, effective January 1, 2022, patients receiving emergency services cannot be balance-billed by any facility, whether in-network or out-of-network. Any charges that exceed what my insurer, BlueCross PPO, has agreed to pay — or that are passed on to me beyond my applicable cost-sharing obligations — would constitute a violation of this federal protection.

Furthermore, charges that are significantly above the Medicare-allowed rate, particularly at multiples of 4x, 12x, or 16x, may constitute excessive or unreasonable billing and are subject to scrutiny under applicable state and federal consumer protection standards.

REQUEST FOR ITEMIZED RECORDS

Pursuant to HIPAA Section 164.524, I hereby request a complete itemized statement of all charges associated with Account #931058 for the date of service February 28, 2024. This should include, at minimum, all CPT codes billed, the description of each service, the quantity of each service rendered, the amount charged for each line item, and any adjustments applied by the insurer or the facility.

Please provide this documentation in writing within the 30-day response window outlined below.

REQUESTED ACTIONS

I respectfully request that Princeton General Hospital take the following steps:

1. Conduct a formal internal review of the three disputed charges identified above.
2. Provide a written explanation and clinical justification for each charge, including the basis for the billed amount relative to the Medicare-allowed rate.
3. Correct any charges found to be erroneous, duplicated, or excessive, and issue a revised bill reflecting those corrections.
4. Provide the complete itemized records requested under HIPAA Section 164.524.
5. Confirm in writing that no balance billing has occurred or will occur in violation of the No Surprises Act.

RESPONSE DEADLINE

I request a written response to this dispute no later than 30 days from the date this letter is received. Please direct your response to the address listed above.

NEXT STEPS IF UNRESOLVED

If I do not receive a satisfactory written response within 30 days, or if the disputed charges are not corrected, I will have no choice but to escalate this matter through the following channels:

- A formal complaint with the New Jersey Office of the Attorney General, Division of Consumer Affairs
- A complaint filed with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB)
- A complaint submitted to the New Jersey Department of Banking and Insurance, which oversees insurance billing practices in this state
- A report to the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) regarding potential violations of the No Surprises Act

I am committed to resolving this matter directly with Princeton General Hospital and hope that a fair and prompt resolution can be reached without further escalation. I am available to discuss this dispute and can be reached at the address provided above.

Respectfully,

Michael K. Patel
Account Number: 931058
[Your Address]
[Your Phone Number]
[Your Email Address]
letter.recipient_block:
To:
Princeton General Hospital — Emergency Department Billing
500 University Blvd
Princeton, NJ 08540

roadmap.title: Request Itemized Bill with CPT Codes
roadmap.detail: Contact Princeton General Hospital's billing department and request a fully itemized bill listing every charge with its CPT code, date of service, and unit price. You are legally entitled to this document and it is the foundation for disputing all 3 flagged charges.

roadmap.title: Review Flagged Charges Against Benchmark Rates
roadmap.detail: CareGap has already identified $3,314.43 in potential savings across 3 flagged line items. Cross-reference each flagged CPT code on your itemized bill against the Medicare reimbursement rates provided in your dispute report to confirm the overcharges before writing your dispute letter.

roadmap.title: Assert Your Rights Under the No Surprises Act
roadmap.detail: Because this is an emergency department bill, you are protected under the No Surprises Act (effective January 1, 2022), which limits out-of-network cost-sharing to in-network rates for emergency services. In your dispute letter, explicitly cite the No Surprises Act and demand that all charges be recalculated at your plan's in-network cost-sharing level.

roadmap.title: Send a Formal Dispute Letter to the Billing Department
roadmap.detail: Draft and send a certified dispute letter to Princeton General Hospital's billing department referencing each flagged charge by CPT code, the benchmark rate, and the No Surprises Act. Request written confirmation of receipt and a response within 30 days, and ask that collections activity be paused while the dispute is under review.

roadmap.title: File a Grievance with Your Insurance Commissioner
roadmap.detail: If the hospital does not resolve the dispute or reduce the flagged charges within 30 days, file a formal complaint with your state insurance commissioner and your insurer's grievance department. For No Surprises Act violations specifically, you can also submit a complaint at federalnosuprises.cms.gov, which carries regulatory weight and often accelerates resolution.

roadmap.title: Consult a Healthcare Billing Advocate or Attorney
roadmap.detail: Given that the disputed amount of $3,314.43 is substantial, engage a certified medical billing advocate or a healthcare attorney if the complaint process does not yield results. Many advocates work on contingency or a percentage of savings recovered, making this a low-risk step that significantly increases your leverage for a bill of this size.

summary:
# What Your Hospital Bill Means

The hospital charged you $2,850 for your emergency room visit, but Medicare says that same visit should only cost about $171—that's a difference of roughly $2,679 that looks like it might be a mistake. Overall, the bill has about $3,314 in charges that seem way too high compared to what insurance companies typically pay for these services. It's worth calling the hospital's billing department to ask them to explain these charges or review them again.
